document_text:
Automatic Cardiac Risk Management
Classification using large-context Electronic
Patients Health Records
Jacopo Vitale1, David Della Morte1, Luca Bacco1,
Mario Merone1, Mark C.H. de Groot2, Saskia Haitjema2,
Leandro Pecchia1, Bram van Es2
1Research Unit of Intelligent Health Technologies, Universit` a Campus
Bio-Medico di Roma, Rome, Italy.
2Central Diagnostic Laboratory, University Medical Center Utrecht,
Utrecht, The Netherlands.
Contributing authors: jacopo.vitale@unicampus.it; bes3@umcutrecht.nl;
Abstract
To overcome the limitations of manual administrative coding in geriatric Car-
diovascular Risk Management, this study introduces an automated classification
framework leveraging unstructured Electronic Health Records (EHRs). Using a
dataset of 3,482 patients, we benchmarked three distinct modeling paradigms
on longitudinal Dutch clinical narratives: classical machine learning baselines,
specialized deep learning architectures optimized for large-context sequences,
and general-purpose generative Large Language Models (LLMs) in a zero-shot
setting. Additionally, we evaluated a late fusion strategy to integrate unstruc-
tured text with structured medication embeddings and anthropometric data.
Our analysis reveals that the custom Transformer architecture outperforms both
traditional methods and generative LLMs, achieving the highest F1-scores and
Matthews Correlation Coefficients. These findings underscore the critical role of
specialized hierarchical attention mechanisms in capturing long-range dependen-
cies within medical texts, presenting a robust, automated alternative to manual
workflows for clinical risk stratification.
1arXiv:2603.09685v1  [cs.CL]  10 Mar 2026

Keywords: Cardiovascular Risk Management, Electronic Health Records,
Hierarchical Transformer, Learning Healthcare System, Large Language Models,
Zero-shot Classification
1 Introduction
In the geriatric population, cardiovascular risk represents a significant challenge due to
the increased prevalence of cardiovascular disease (CVD) with advancing age [27, 3].
Older adults often exhibit a complex clinical profile, where multiple factors including
comorbidities, age-related physiological changes, frailty, and polypharmacy synergis-
tically contribute to an elevated cardiovascular risk [12]. Although clinical guidelines
exist to support healthcare professionals in managing cardiovascular risk, they are
not always consistently implemented in routine practice and may become outdated
as new evidence emerges [7, 5]. This results in suboptimal screening and manage-
ment of cardiovascular risk factors, ultimately contributing to avoidable morbidity and
mortality.
A Learning Healthcare System (LHS) provides a promising framework to address
these limitations by continuously analyzing data from routine clinical care, identifying
gaps and inefficiencies, and translating new evidence into actionable improvements in
practice. First introduced in 2007, the LHS was conceptualized as a system in which
care delivery and evidence generation are tightly integrated in a continuous cycle of
learning and improvement.
Formally, an LHS is defined as a healthcare system that systematically learns from
clinical experience by generating and applying evidence to support medical decision-
making, foster innovation, and enhance the quality, safety, and efficiency of care
delivery [13, 23]. Within the LHS framework, Electronic Health Records (EHRs) serve
as the fundamental infrastructure that enables the transformation of routine clinical
practice into systematic knowledge. EHRs function as a centralized access point for
2

patient data and include both structured information, such as laboratory values, and
unstructured content, such as prescribed medications, medical notes, and information
related to appointments and hospitalizations. By leveraging the data routinely cap-
tured in EHRs, it becomes possible to automatically accumulate longitudinal patient
information without the need for dedicated follow-up. This results in the creation
of a dynamic and scalable database that supports predictive modeling and real-time
risk stratification. Furthermore, the integration of EHR data facilitates the early
identification and continuous monitoring of high-risk individuals, thereby enabling
the implementation of personalized preventive and therapeutic strategies [9, 10]. In
2016, the Center for Circulatory Health at the University Medical Center Utrecht
(UMCU), in the Netherlands, launched the Utrecht Cardiovascular Cohort – Car-
diovascular Risk Management (UCC-CVRM) initiative, which constitutes one of the
first operationalizations of a cardiovascular LHS. Following the guidelines set by the
Utrecht Cardiovascular Cohort, the cardiovascular LHS aims to uniformly assess and
record risk indicators for all patients included in the Cardiovascular Risk Management
(CVRM) [1]. This approach allows data generated from routine check-ups to be easily
analyzed, enabling continuous improvement in the delivery of healthcare services [1].
At UMCU patients are considered eligible to CVRM via codes in their EHR namely,
agenda codes , that denote specific types of appointments, consultations, or healthcare
services. This eligibility assignment system has significant limitations, including the
fact that it does not account for the patient’s physiological parameters or clinical his-
tory, as these codes can change over time and may not accurately reflect the patients’
clinical history. Moreover, the assigment code is assigned manually by a nurse leading
to selection errors.
This study leverages the extensive EHR database of the UMCU, which includes a
wide range of patient information such as demographics, medical history, diagnoses,
treatments, and laboratory results, representing a valuable resource for enhancing
3

CVRM. Specifically, the contributions of this work are threefold: (i) To collect and
preprocess highly unstructured data from the UMCU EHR system, transforming it
into a structured dataset suitable for downstream analysis; (ii) To develop an auto-
mated strategy for assessing patient eligibility for the CVRM program, with particular
emphasis on the integration of clinical history data; (iii) To explore and evaluate vari-
ous model architectures and techniques capable of handling long-context information,
in order to improve the identification of high-risk patients.
2 Dataset
From UMCU database we retrieved all record from 3482 patients EHRs collected
from the geriatric outpatient clinic. Each patient’s medical record is assigned an iden-
tification code based on the type of appointment or visit, without considering any
physiological parameters. Among the twenty-six possible codes that can be assigned,
four designate the automatic enrollement to the CVR management programme and
identified as eligible.
A detailed summary of the dataset characteristics is presented in Table 1. Patients
were categorized into two classes based on their eligibility for the CVRM program:
class 0 denotes non-eligible patients, while class 1 denotes eligible patients. As shown
in Table 1, the dataset is strongly imbalanced with respect to the class distribution.
Table 1 Demographic and class distribution of the dataset.
Characteristic Value
Male samples 1871 (53.73%)
Female samples 1611 (46.27%)
Age (mean ±SD) M: 74.52 ±9.05, F: 74.40 ±9.51
Class 0 2808 (80.61%)
Class 1 674 (19.39%)
Total number of samples 3482
4

Each patient has an undetermined number of medical text or medications based
on his/her own medical history.
Electronic Health Record

Medications
Fig. 1 Graphical representation of an EHR. Highlighted items are the one used in this study, the
not highlighted one are present but discarded.
2.1 Consult texts
In a geriatric outpatient clinic, a consultation report (consultverslag) serves as a
structured medical document summarizing the specialist’s assessment and recommen-
dations following a patient visit. These reports include a multidimensional evaluation,
addressing the patient’s primary complaint, comorbidities, cognitive status, functional
ability, and psychosocial factors.
The document follows a standardized format, beginning with the specialty and
consultation type, followed by patient demographics, medical history, and present-
ing symptoms. A clinical examination includes cognitive screening (e.g., MMSE), fall
risk assessment, and relevant diagnostic findings. The conclusion section presents a
differential diagnosis, considering age-related conditions such as dementia, Parkin-
son disease, or polypharmacy-related effects. The report outlines a treatment plan,
including referrals to other specialists, medication adjustments, and recommendations
for follow-up care. The structured format ensures continuity of care and facilitates
interdisciplinary collaboration in managing the complex needs of older adults. In our
5

dataset, we have multiple consultation reports per patient, each associated with a
specific date allowing for longitudinal tracking of patient progress and adjustments in
care.
2.2 Anthropometrics
The EHR contains a variety of anthropometric data, including age, gender, and poten-
tially other measurements such as BMI. However, due to the unstructured nature of
the EHR, as highlighted in the literature, extracting and organizing these data points
into a usable format presents significant challenges. In our dataset, we focused on
using age and gender because these variables are typically well-structured and eas-
ier to extract. Other anthropometric measures, such as BMI, were not consistently
available or structured in a way that would allow for reliable inclusion in the analy-
sis. The difficulties in processing and polishing EHR data to a usable state limit the
integration of more detailed anthropometric information, despite its potential value in
patient assessment.
2.3 Medication Processing
Medications represent a critical unstructured feature, originally recorded in the EHRs
by their trade or generic names. To ensure semantic consistency and resolve vari-
ability between different denominations for the same active ingredient, all medication
entries were mapped to the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification
system [26], developed by the World Health Organization. For each patient, the
pharmacological history spanning the observation year was transformed from raw
prescription names into a list of decompressed ATC code descriptions. This standard-
ization step converts heterogeneous drug data into a unified textual format, serving
as the input for the semantic embedding and aggregation pipeline detailed in the
Methodology.
6

3 Methodology
Data from consultations, medications, and anthropometric measurements acquired for
a single year were selected from patients’ EHRs, utilizing the most recent medical
appointment as the reference time-point. To systematically evaluate the impact of
integrating these distinct modalities across all our evaluated models, we investigated
two modeling strategies: a unimodal text-only approach and a multimodal late fusion
approach (Figure 2) [25, 19]. The text-only strategy serves as a baseline, relying solely
on the text encoders to classify the clinical narratives. Conversely, the late fusion
strategy processes the unstructured text independently and concatenates the resulting
feature representation with the structured data vectors (medications and anthropo-
metrics) just prior to the final classification head. We selected this specific fusion
architecture because it allows the core sequence-modeling networks to be utilized with-
out requiring complex internal modifications for multimodal processing, while still
empirically maintaining robust predictive performance [18].
To operationalize this late fusion strategy, the supplementary modalities required
mathematical representation. To incorporate the pharmacological data, we lever-
aged the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) codes already recorded in the
patient EHRs [26]. We extracted the decompressed textual description associated
with each code to capture the semantic nuance of the prescribed treatments. Subse-
quently, these descriptive strings were converted into numerical embeddings using the
BioLORD-2023 model, a Sentence-Transformer [21, 14]. This model was selected for
its pre-training on the UMLS medical ontology, which includes descriptions of ATC
codes, enabling it to map semantically similar drugs to proximate coordinates within
the vector space [2]. By feeding the ATC descriptions into this model, we obtained
768-dimensional embeddings, which were subsequently condensed into a single rep-
resentative vector per patient to serve as the structured input for the late fusion
layer.
7

We benchmarked our proposed architecture against three distinct families of model-
ing paradigms, applying both the text-only and late fusion strategies where applicable.
First, to establish traditional machine learning baselines, we employed a stratified
dummy classifier and a Linear Support Vector Classifier (LinearSVC) trained on Term
Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) representations [15, 16]. Second,
to evaluate the efficacy of attention mechanisms against convolutional approaches for
sequential medical text, we implemented a custom one-dimensional ResNet [8]. Finally,
we compared our supervised architectures against general-purpose LLMs applied in a
zero-shot classification setting [28, 11].
To process the longitudinal consultation narratives, we developed a cus-
tom encoder-only Transformer architecture designed specifically for large-context
sequences. The input text is first encoded using a pre-trained tokenizer, mapping each
token ID to a 512-dimensional embedding space. The sequence is padded or trun-
cated to a maximum length of 8192 tokens. To track token positions within the input
text, a rotary positional embedding [22] is applied. For the final classification mecha-
nism, we investigated two distinct aggregation strategies. The first approach utilizes a
Classification (CLS) token [4] prepended to the embedded sequence. During the back-
propagation phase, this randomly initialized token inflates with global classification
information; after the Transformer Encoder stack, only the CLS token is pooled and
passed to a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to output class predictions, as detailed in
Fig. 2. Alternatively, we explored a Global Average Pooling strategy, where the out-
put token sequence is simply averaged to form the final representation, eliminating
the need for a CLS token.

Not EligibleMLPFig. 2 Schematic overview of the Hierarchical Transformer classification pipeline. The architecture
processes concatenated consults via BPE tokenization and hierarchical encoding, utilizing CLS Clas-
sification Token (orange arrows) or Global Average Pooling (purple arrows) strategies before late
fusion with anthropometric data (or not).
Each Transformer Encoder layer is composed of a Hierarchical Attention Layer
and a Feed-Forward network layer, every output of those layer is added via skip con-
nection and layer-normalised as explained in [24]. The token sequence flows into the
Transformer encoder where hierarchical attention is computed as shown in [29]. This
approach allows large context-length text to be processed with acceptable computa-
tional time and memory complexity. This modified version of attention requires that
the input is padded to a power of two, as attention is computed hierarchically on token
pairs, prioritizing local dependencies over distant ones [29]. In parallel, we implemented
a custom ResNet-1D architecture tailored to sequential representations, providing a
convolutional baseline for comparison with Transformer-based methods.
The hyperparameters and structural configurations detailed in Table 2 were
selected following a grid search constrained by our available GPU resources. We
9

observed that variations among the top-performing configurations yielded no statisti-
cally significant improvements; therefore, for clarity and brevity, we report only the
hyperparameter set that achieved the highest validation metrics.

3.1 Training and Optimization
All deep learning models were trained using the AMSGrad [20] optimizer. Given the
unbalanced nature of the dataset, we employed a Stratified 5-fold cross-validation
strategy. This ensures that the proportion of samples for each class is preserved in
each fold, providing a robust evaluation metric. The specific training hyperparameters
are detailed in Table 3.
10

Table 3 Training & Optimization Configuration . These hyperparameters are shared across
all model variations in the experimental setup.
Parameter Value
Epochs 30
Batch Size 12
Learning Rate 3 ×10−5
Weight Decay 1 ×10−4
Optimizer Flag Adam (AMSGrad)
Data Split Strategy
Cross-Validation Stratified K-Fold
K-Fold Splits 5
Test Size 0 .2 (20%)
Seed 42
Finally, we evaluated few LLMs in a zero-shot classification setting with a two-
prompt approach with the first prompt for translation from Dutch to English and the
second prompt to extract the binary label, where we add an extract of the CVRM
guidelines to the system prompt. The LLMs used were GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-
4.1 and GPT-4.1-mini1. Before the API is called the consult texts are de-identified
using DEDUCE [17]2. We prepend the consult texts with the age and the gender of
the patient.
Note that the CVRM guidelines are over 400 pages. In the spirit of this approach
we prompted GPT 5.2 to make an extractive summary of the guidelines regarding
the core CVRM factors, see appendix A. We append this extractive summary to the
system prompt.
Prompt Group :Two-step setup

4 Results and Discussion
Table 4 reports the performance of all models using consult texts only as input, while
the late-fusion approach, which combines textual data with patient anthropometric
features, is summarized in Table 5. Both tables report F1-score, Precision, Recall, and
12

Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) as evaluation metrics (reported below).

As shown in Table 4 and Table 5, hierarchical Transformer models consistently
outperform traditional machine learning baselines and state-of-the-art zero-shot LLMs
in both text-only and late-fusion settings, demonstrating that their architecture
effectively captures the complex structure of clinical texts. In the text-only setting
(Table 4), the classification token variant (†) achieved an F1-score of 92.48% with an
MCC of 0.758, while the average pooling variant (‡) yielded slightly lower yet still
13

strong performance (F1-score: 91.02%, MCC: 0.730). When anthropometric features
were incorporated via late fusion (Table 5), both variants maintained top performance,
with F1-scores ranging from 89.73% to 91.48% and MCC values between 0.646 and
0.718.
Table 5 Performance of CVRM prediction based on consult texts and anthropometric measures as
input. Values are shown as mean (std).
Models F1 (%) Precision (%) Recall (%) MCC⊕
Dummy Classifier 50.76 (0.0) 50.75 (0.0) 50.78 (0.0) 0.015 (0.0)
Linear SVM△86.30 (0.56) 82.97 (0.54) 92.87 (0.43) 0.752 (0.010)
H-transformer1D†92.48 (0.67) 92.48 (0.67) 92.48 (0.67) 0.758 (0.024)
H-transformer1D‡91.02 (1.11) 91.02 (1.11) 91.02 (1.11) 0.730 (0.028)
ResNet1D 85.48 (0.69) 85.48 (0.69) 85.48 (0.69) 0.565 (0.023)
⊕: Matthews Correlation Coefficient; †: Hierarchical Transformer with Classification Token Pooling;
‡: Hierarchical Transformer with Average Pooling; △: Support Vector Classifier with Linear Kernel;
Although the choice of pooling strategy has a minimal impact on overall perfor-
mance, the classification token-based variant consistently achieves the highest scores,
suggesting that this strategy is better suited to capturing the most informative con-
tent present in medical consultation texts. Among the evaluated baselines, the SVM
achieved strong performance across the considered metrics. Despite its relatively sim-
ple architecture, SVM yielded results that were only moderately lower than those
obtained by our proposed model, confirming its effectiveness in this clinical prediction
task. This result is consistent with previous evidence highlighting the robustness of
SVM in clinical and healthcare applications, particularly in settings characterized by
limited data availability and high-dimensional feature spaces. Prior to the widespread
adoption of transformer-based architectures, SVMs were considered a strong method-
ological standard in clinical machine learning and continue to represent a competitive
baseline in such scenarios [6]. In contrast, ResNet shows substantially lower perfor-
mance, particularly when trained using only medical consultations. This suggests that
14

deep learning architectures may struggle in clinical tasks when training data are lim-
ited. Such models typically rely on large amounts of labeled data to effectively learn
task-relevant representations, a requirement that is often difficult to satisfy in real-
world clinical settings. Indeed, this trend is even more pronounced for LLMs evaluated
in a zero-shot configuration, which exhibited the weakest performance. These find-
ings suggest that, in the absence of domain adaptation or fine-tuning, LLMs may
lack adequate understanding of the clinical context required for reliable prediction.
Nonetheless, all models achieved performance levels superior to the dummy classifier,
demonstrating their capacity to extract meaningful information from both structured
and unstructured data. All in all, the superior performance of the H-transformer-
1D highlights the importance of hierarchical attention when processing the complex,
multi-dimensional evaluations typical of geriatric consultation reports. Unlike stan-
dard models, the hierarchical approach efficiently captures long-range dependencies
within the clinical history, which is essential given that geriatric patients often present
with multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy that synergistically contribute to
cardiovascular risk.
5 Conclusion
This study demonstrates that deep learning architectures can successfully automate
the identification of cardiovascular risk management eligibility using unstructured
electronic health records. The proposed custom Hierarchical Transformer effectively
handled longitudinal clinical narratives, yielding higher classification performance
compared to both traditional machine learning baselines and general-purpose Large
Language Models (LLMs) applied in a zero-shot setting.
Our findings indicate that while general-purpose generative models offer broad
capabilities, they currently lack the precision required for this specific non-English
15

clinical task without explicit supervision. Although evaluating fine-tuned, domain-
specific clinical LLMs presents an interesting future avenue, the choice to develop
a bespoke encoder architecture was driven by a clear operational rationale. Strict
data privacy regulations necessitate that sensitive patient EHRs remain within secure,
compute-constrained, on-premise hospital environments. In such infrastructural set-
tings, fine-tuning massive autoregressive generative models is often computationally
prohibitive. Conversely, our proposed Transformer, while architecturally sophisticated
in its attention mechanism, maintains a significantly reduced training footprint. It is
inherently optimized for sequence classification rather than text generation, making it
both highly efficient to train from scratch and practically deployable. Consequently,
task-specific encoder architectures remain a necessary and pragmatic solution for
reliable risk stratification.
By mitigating the selection errors associated with manual administrative coding,
this automated approach supports the operational goals of a Learning Healthcare
System. Future implementation of such models in routine practice offers a scalable,
privacy-compliant method to utilize accumulated patient data for continuous quality
improvement and more accurate geriatric risk assessment.
Declarations
Ethics approval and consent to participate
The UMCU quality assurance research officer confirmed under project number 22U-
0292 that this study does not fall under the scope of the Dutch Medical Research
Involving Human Subjects Act (WMO) and therefore does not require approval from
an accredited medical ethics committee. The study was performed compliant with
local legislation and regulations. All patient data were deidentified in compliance with
the European Union General Data Protection Regulation, and as a result, written
informed consent was not required by the UMCU ethical committee.
16

Availability of data and materials
The datasets generated and/or analysed during the current study are not publicly
available due to potential privacy-sensitive information, but are available from the cor-
responding author upon reasonable request and local institutional approval. Research
code is publicly available on GitHub, via the code repository for this work.
Competing interests
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
Funding
The work was supported in part by the ODIN Project, as part of the European Union’s
Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation Program, under Grant 101017331.

17

A GPT summary of CVRM guidelines
Prompt:
I want to use ChatGPT to extract labels from patient consults regarding cardiovascular
risk management. To guide the model I want to include guidelines but these are expansive,
with over 400 pages of text. Could you extract from these guidelines the core points
regarding co-morbidities, symptoms, prior history, risk scores and other factors that
are likely included in these consults?
Keep it brief. No extra explanatory text, just an extractive summary.
Response:
## Core CVRM factors commonly documented in patient consults
### Demographics
- Age
- Sex
### Symptoms / Clinical presentation
- Chest pain / angina
- Dyspnea
- Palpitations (e.g. atrial fibrillation)
- Neurological deficits / TIA / stroke symptoms
- Claudication (peripheral arterial disease)
- Signs of heart failure
### Prior history (established disease)
18

- Documented atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
(coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, stroke/TIA,
peripheral arterial disease, aortic aneurysm)
- Diabetes mellitus (type 1 or 2; duration, complications)
- Chronic kidney disease (eGFR, albuminuria)
- Familial hypercholesterolemia
- Atrial fibrillation
- Heart failure
- Hypertension
- Prior revascularization or vascular procedures
### Risk factors (anamnestic)
- Smoking status (current/former, pack-years)
- Family history of premature cardiovascular disease
- Diet quality
- Physical inactivity / sedentary behavior
- Alcohol use
- Psychosocial stress, depression, low socioeconomic status
### Risk factors (clinical / measurements)
- Systolic blood pressure
- Body mass index (BMI)
- Waist circumference
### Laboratory factors
- LDL-cholesterol
- Non-HDL-cholesterol
- Total cholesterol
19

- HDL-cholesterol
- Triglycerides
- Fasting glucose
- Serum creatinine / eGFR
- Urine albumin{creatinine ratio
### Risk scores
- SCORE2 (ages 40{70)
- SCORE2-OP (ages 70{90)
- SMART2 / SMART-REACH (established cardiovascular disease)
- DIAL2 (diabetes mellitus)
### Risk modifiers / additional factors
- Coronary artery calcium score
- Psychosocial factors
- Ethnic background
- Chronic inflammatory diseases
(rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis)
- COPD
- Gout
- HIV infection
- Inflammatory bowel disease
- Obstructive sleep apnea
- History of pre-eclampsia or pregnancy-related hypertension
- Severe psychiatric disorders
- Prior chemo- or radiotherapy
### Treatment-related context often noted
20

- Current antihypertensive therapy
- Current lipid-lowering therapy (statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors)
- Blood pressure target attainment
- LDL-C target attainment
- Medication adherence
- Polypharmacy and frailty (especially in older adults)

structure.title: Automatic Cardiac Risk Management Classification using large-context Electronic Patients Health Records
structure.summary: This partial document discusses an automated framework for cardiac risk management using electronic health records. The main points covered include the introduction of a classification framework to overcome manual coding limitations, the benchmarking of different modeling paradigms, and the evaluation of a late fusion strategy to integrate unstructured text with structured data. The study finds that a custom Transformer architecture outperforms traditional methods and generative language models, highlighting the importance of specialized attention mechanisms in capturing long-range dependencies within medical texts. The document presents a robust automated alternative to manual workflows for clinical risk stratification.

nodes.title: Abstract
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the development of an automated classification framework for cardiac risk management in geriatric patients using large-context Electronic Health Records (EHRs). The study introduces a framework that leverages unstructured EHRs to overcome the limitations of manual administrative coding and evaluates the performance of different machine learning models, including classical machine learning, deep learning architectures, and Large Language Models. The document highlights the importance of a Learning Healthcare System (LHS) in continuously analyzing data from routine clinical care and translating new evidence into improvements in practice. The main points covered include the challenges of cardiovascular risk management in older adults, the potential of LHS to address these challenges, and the evaluation of different modeling paradigms for automated classification using EHRs.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the management of cardiovascular risk in the geriatric population, highlighting the challenges and limitations of current clinical guidelines and practices. It introduces the concept of a Learning Healthcare System (LHS) and the role of Electronic Health Records (EHRs) in transforming routine clinical practice into systematic knowledge. The document also describes the Utrecht Cardiovascular Cohort – Cardiovascular Risk Management (UCC-CVRM) initiative and its aim to uniformly assess and record risk indicators for patients. The study focuses on leveraging EHR data to develop an automated strategy for assessing patient eligibility for the CVRM program, exploring various model architectures, and improving the identification of high-risk patients. The dataset used in the study consists of EHRs from 3482 patients, with a strong class imbalance between eligible and non-eligible patients. The document also provides an overview of the EHR structure, including consultation reports, medical history, and medications, which are used to develop a structured dataset for downstream analysis.

nodes.title: Consult texts
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the components and structure of Electronic Health Records (EHRs), specifically focusing on consult texts, anthropometrics, and medication processing. It highlights the importance of standardized formats in EHRs, such as consultation reports, which provide a comprehensive overview of a patient's medical history, symptoms, and treatment plans. The document also touches on the challenges of extracting and organizing anthropometric data due to the unstructured nature of EHRs. Additionally, it explains the process of standardizing medication data using the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system to ensure semantic consistency and facilitate analysis.

nodes.title: Anthropometrics
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the composition and processing of a dataset derived from Electronic Health Records (EHRs). The main points covered include the longitudinal tracking of patient progress through multiple consultation reports, the extraction and organization of anthropometric data such as age and gender, and the standardization of medication data using the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system to ensure semantic consistency and resolve variability in medication names.

nodes.title: Medication Processing
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the composition and processing of electronic health records (EHRs) data, focusing on three main areas: consultation reports for longitudinal patient tracking, anthropometric data such as age and gender, and medication processing. It highlights the challenges of extracting and organizing data from unstructured EHRs, particularly for anthropometric measures and medication information. The document also describes the standardization of medication data using the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system to ensure semantic consistency and enable further analysis.

structure.title: Methodology
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the methodology used to evaluate the impact of integrating different modalities, such as text, medications, and anthropometric measurements, on clinical narrative classification. It describes two modeling strategies: a unimodal text-only approach and a multimodal late fusion approach. The document also outlines the process of converting pharmacological data into numerical embeddings using the BioLORD-2023 model and the architecture of a custom encoder-only Transformer designed for large-context sequences. Additionally, it mentions the benchmarking of the proposed architecture against traditional machine learning baselines and general-purpose language models, as well as the use of a custom ResNet-1D architecture and a stratified 5-fold cross-validation strategy for training and optimization. The main points covered include data selection, modeling strategies, data representation, architecture design, and training and optimization techniques.

nodes.title: Training and Optimization
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the experimental setup and results of various machine learning models, including Hierarchical Transformers, ResNet-1D, and LinearSVC, for a classification task. It presents a summary of the model architectures and hyperparameters used, as well as the training and optimization configurations. The document also touches on the evaluation of large language models (LLMs) in a zero-shot classification setting, using a two-prompt approach and extractive summarization of guidelines. The main points covered include model architecture, hyperparameter tuning, training and optimization strategies, and the evaluation of LLMs in a specific classification task.

structure.title: Results and Discussion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the use of deep learning architectures for automating the identification of cardiovascular risk management eligibility using unstructured electronic health records. It presents the results of an experiment that evaluated the performance of various models, including Hierarchical Transformers, Support Vector Machines, and Large Language Models, in a zero-shot setting. The document also describes the training and optimization configuration, data split strategy, and evaluation metrics used in the experiment. The main points covered include the comparison of model performances, the importance of hierarchical attention in processing clinical narratives, and the limitations of general-purpose Large Language Models in clinical prediction tasks.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the challenges of using deep learning architectures in clinical tasks, particularly when training data is limited. It highlights the importance of hierarchical attention in processing complex clinical data and presents a custom Hierarchical Transformer model that outperforms traditional machine learning baselines and general-purpose Large Language Models. The document also touches on the limitations of general-purpose models in clinical tasks, the need for task-specific encoder architectures, and the potential of automated risk stratification in supporting Learning Healthcare Systems. Additionally, it mentions the study's compliance with ethics regulations and data privacy laws, including the deidentification of patient data and adherence to the European Union General Data Protection Regulation. The main points covered include the evaluation of deep learning models in clinical tasks, the development of a custom Hierarchical Transformer model, and the discussion of ethics and data privacy considerations.

structure.title: Declarations
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the development of a bespoke encoder architecture for clinical task automation, driven by the need for strict data privacy and computational efficiency. It highlights the benefits of using a task-specific encoder architecture for reliable risk stratification, particularly in geriatric risk assessment. The document also touches on the operational goals of a Learning Healthcare System, the mitigation of selection errors associated with manual administrative coding, and the potential for scalable and privacy-compliant implementation of automated models in routine practice. Additionally, it includes declarations on ethics approval and consent to participate, confirming compliance with local legislation and regulations, including the European Union General Data Protection Regulation.

nodes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the development of a bespoke encoder architecture for clinical task automation, driven by the need for strict data privacy and computational efficiency. It highlights the benefits of using a task-specific encoder architecture for reliable risk stratification, particularly in geriatric risk assessment, and its potential for scalable and privacy-compliant implementation in routine practice. The document also touches on the ethical considerations, confirming compliance with local legislation and regulations, and the deidentification of patient data in accordance with the European Union General Data Protection Regulation.

nodes.title: Availability of data and materials
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the availability of data and materials, competing interests, funding, and authors' contributions in a research study. It mentions that the datasets are not publicly available due to privacy concerns but can be obtained from the corresponding author, and the research code is available on GitHub. The document also declares no competing interests among the authors and acknowledges funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation Program. Additionally, it outlines the specific contributions of each author to the study, including conceptualization, methodology, data curation, writing, and supervision.

structure.title: A GPT summary of CVRM guidelines
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the core points of Cardiovascular Risk Management (CVRM) guidelines, focusing on key factors commonly documented in patient consults, including demographics, symptoms, and prior history, to guide a GPT model in extracting labels from patient consults.

nodes.title: Core CVRM factors commonly documented in patient consults
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the core points of Cardiovascular Risk Management (CVRM) guidelines, focusing on key factors commonly documented in patient consults, including demographics, symptoms, and prior medical history, to guide a ChatGPT model in extracting labels from patient consults regarding cardiovascular risk management.

nodes.title: Demographics
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the core points of Cardiovascular Risk Management (CVRM) guidelines, focusing on key factors commonly documented in patient consults, including demographics, symptoms, and prior history, to guide a GPT model in extracting labels from patient consults.

nodes.title: Symptoms / Clinical presentation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the core points of Cardiovascular Risk Management (CVRM) guidelines, focusing on key factors commonly found in patient consults, including demographics, symptoms, and prior medical history, to guide a GPT model in extracting relevant labels.

nodes.title: Prior history (established disease)
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the core points of Cardiovascular Risk Management (CVRM) guidelines, focusing on factors commonly documented in patient consults. The main points covered include demographics, symptoms and clinical presentation, prior history of established diseases, risk factors (both anamnestic and clinical/measurement-based), and laboratory factors. These factors encompass a range of elements such as age, sex, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, neurological deficits, prior history of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, smoking status, family history, diet quality, physical inactivity, systolic blood pressure, body mass index, LDL-cholesterol, and non-HDL-cholesterol, among others.

nodes.title: Risk factors (anamnestic)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers various medical conditions and risk factors related to cardiovascular disease, including documented atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, and other heart-related conditions. It also lists anamnestic risk factors such as smoking status, family history, diet quality, physical inactivity, and psychosocial stress, as well as clinical and laboratory factors like blood pressure, body mass index, and cholesterol levels.

nodes.title: Risk factors (clinical / measurements)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers various medical conditions and risk factors related to cardiovascular disease, including documented atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, and other heart-related conditions. It also lists anamnestic risk factors such as smoking status, family history, diet quality, physical inactivity, and psychosocial stress. Additionally, the document includes clinical and laboratory measurements like systolic blood pressure, body mass index, waist circumference, and cholesterol levels, which are used to assess cardiovascular risk.

nodes.title: Laboratory factors
nodes.summary: The partial document covers various aspects of cardiovascular disease risk assessment, including documented atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, and other health conditions. It also lists anamnestic risk factors such as smoking status, family history, diet quality, physical inactivity, and psychosocial stress. Additionally, the document includes clinical and laboratory measurements like blood pressure, body mass index, lipid profiles, and glucose levels. Risk scores, including SCORE2, SCORE2-OP, SMART2, and DIAL2, are also mentioned, along with risk modifiers like coronary artery calcium score, ethnic background, and chronic inflammatory diseases. The document also notes treatment-related context and other factors that may influence cardiovascular disease risk.

nodes.title: Risk scores
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be related to cardiovascular risk assessment and management. It covers various laboratory tests, including HDL-cholesterol, triglycerides, fasting glucose, and serum creatinine, as well as risk scores such as SCORE2 and SMART2. The document also lists risk modifiers and additional factors, including coronary artery calcium score, psychosocial factors, ethnic background, and chronic inflammatory diseases. Furthermore, it mentions treatment-related context and various comorbidities, such as COPD, gout, and HIV infection, that may influence cardiovascular risk. Overall, the document seems to provide a comprehensive framework for evaluating and managing cardiovascular risk in patients.

nodes.title: Risk modifiers / additional factors
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be related to cardiovascular health and risk assessment, covering key points such as lipid profiles (HDL-cholesterol, triglycerides), glucose and kidney function tests (fasting glucose, serum creatinine, eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio), and various risk scores (SCORE2, SCORE2-OP, SMART2, DIAL2). It also lists risk modifiers and additional factors, including coronary artery calcium score, psychosocial factors, ethnic background, and presence of chronic inflammatory diseases. Furthermore, it touches upon treatment-related context, although the specifics are not detailed in this portion of the document.

nodes.title: Treatment-related context often noted
nodes.summary: The partial document covers various aspects of cardiovascular health, including laboratory tests such as HDL-cholesterol, triglycerides, and fasting glucose, as well as risk scores like SCORE2 and SMART2. It also discusses risk modifiers and additional factors like coronary artery calcium score, psychosocial factors, and chronic inflammatory diseases. The document touches on treatment-related context, including current antihypertensive therapy, lipid-lowering therapy, and medication adherence. Additionally, it references several studies and research papers related to cardiovascular care, quality control, and research, highlighting the importance of uniform data collection and clinical concept embeddings in improving patient outcomes.

nodes.title: References
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses various aspects of cardiovascular health, including current antihypertensive and lipid-lowering therapies, blood pressure and LDL-C target attainment, medication adherence, and polypharmacy and frailty, particularly in older adults. It also references numerous studies and research papers on topics such as uniform data collection in clinical practice, clinical concept embeddings, heart disease and stroke statistics, and the application of artificial intelligence and machine learning in healthcare. The document appears to be a collection of references and notes on cardiovascular health and the use of technology in healthcare, with a focus on optimizing treatment and improving patient outcomes.
